prompts.name: Conversation Closeout + Executable Harness-Build Sprint Mapper
prompts.moment: Conversation Closeout + Ordered Copy-Panel Sprint Mapper
prompts.sprintPathRole: Convert chat into implementation launch order and assign harness/application builders
prompts.useThisWhen: A chat contains decisions, unfinished work, or harness gaps that must become self-contained implementation sprints.
prompts.doNotUseWhen: A different prompt more directly owns the requested moment.
prompts.inspectFirst: Current conversation and known repo context.
prompts.expectedOutput: Launch order, executable build panels with named harness/application owners, and a supporting sprint map.
prompts.nextStep: Launch the first implementation panel; do not stop at the map.
prompts.acceptanceGate: Every identified harness gap has an executable build owner or cited evidence that no build is needed.
prompts.text:
We covered a lot and only progressed partway. Convert this chat into a sprint map and executable launch pack.
Preserve only context that prevents rework. Do not summarize every topic equally.
SOURCE OF TRUTH
Use the best available chat context and, when repo access exists, verify current repo, branch, PR, worktree, test, validator, artifact, and CI state.
Prefer repository evidence over remembered chat context. Mark stale or uncertain claims explicitly.
OUTPUT ORDER — LAUNCH ORDER FIRST
Analyze the material before writing, but the first substantive section must be LAUNCH ORDER.
1. LAUNCH ORDER
Put the launch order at the very top immediately after the title.
Name the first chat, every dependency gate, each parallel group, and the final convergence chat.
Number and title the prompts exactly as they will appear below.
The prompt panels must be displayed in the exact same order as this launch order.
Do not describe one launch order and present the panels in another order.
Do not include an alternate display order, category order, or convenience order.
Keep prompts in the same parallel group contiguous under that group.
State: one prompt panel goes into one new chat.
2. COMPACT COORDINATION PREAMBLE
Place one short readable preamble after the launch order and before the panels.
Include:
- mission
- repo and primary local path if known
- current branch, PR, and verified floor if known
- wave order and hard dependencies
- safe parallel lanes
- principal collision risks
- proof ceiling and critical safety boundary
The preamble is for orientation only.
Do not output a shared chat preamble that must be copied separately.
Every prompt panel must contain all context required to run that chat by itself.
3. NEXT CHAT PANELS
Immediately after the preamble, emit every next-chat prompt in its own separate writing block or copyable panel.
Each panel must have its own copy control.
Display panels in the exact order already stated in LAUNCH ORDER.
Do not put all prompts in one code fence.
Do not put two prompts in one panel.
Do not place commentary, analysis, or summaries between panels.
Do not require the operator to combine text from multiple sections.
Each panel must be self-contained and executable.
Every panel must include:
- banner and sprint name
- repo, local path if known, branch, PR, wave, and lane
- dependencies and safe parallel work
- owned scope and forbidden scope
- expected artifacts and mission
- read-first files and compact preflight
- numbered tasks
- sprint-specific safety
- validation order
- commit, push, and PR contract when repo mutation is intended
- proof level, evidence, and proof ceiling
- exact final response contract
- exact next command
- an explicit instruction to BUILD, REPAIR, VALIDATE, or RESEARCH the owned surface
- when repo mutation is intended: inspect, implement, validate, commit, push, and open or update the PR
- never leave required harness construction as an unassigned description
Use a short title immediately above each panel.
4. HARNESS AND APPLICATION FACTORING PASS
Before assigning sprint ownership, classify every lingering requirement into one primary surface:
- harness spine: run context, artifact registry, schemas, validators, fixtures, workflows, proof gates
- agent harness: skills, capabilities, triggers, routing, instruction surfaces, manifests, MCP/tools, hooks, context injection
- conventional application logic: domain models, services, state machines, adapters, persistence, UI, launchers, deployment orchestration
- integration seam: interfaces between harness, agent routing, and application logic
- docs/reporting
- validation
- runtime proof
- research/design
For every skill, capability, or trigger, decide: keep, create, split, merge, retire, or rewire.
Record for each:
- owner
- deterministic activation condition
- inputs and outputs
- preconditions and forbidden conditions
- guardrails
- tests and proof level
- files or manifests likely to change
Use these boundaries:
- skills describe reusable workflow guidance
- capabilities expose reusable operations
- triggers deterministically route conditions to a skill, capability, or workflow
- application logic remains in code and domain contracts, not hidden in prompts
- prompts may orchestrate work but must not become the only implementation of product behavior
Create separate factoring sprints when agent harness work and application logic would otherwise collide.
5. HARNESS BUILD OWNERSHIP
Do not stop at describing, classifying, or mapping the harness.
When the factoring pass finds missing, partial, stale, or unvalidated harness spine or agent-harness work, the launch order MUST include one or more executable build sprints that direct AI agents to implement it in the repository.
Every required harness surface must have an owner panel or explicit repository evidence that the current implementation is sufficient.
A harness-build panel must instruct the assigned agent to:
- inspect existing repo rules, harness files, validators, workflows, manifests, hooks, and generated-output policy before inventing
- create or repair the owned harness files, schemas, fixtures, skills, capabilities, triggers, workflows, hooks, and integration seams
- keep application behavior in code and domain contracts rather than prompts
- run the exact harness and repository validators
- commit coherent changes
- push normally and open or update the intended PR when the environment allows
- report exact artifacts, validation evidence, commit SHA, PR state, proof level, proof ceiling, and next command
Separate harness-spine, agent-harness, and application-logic build sprints when they would collide in the same files or ownership lane.
Research-only panels may precede implementation only when a real unknown blocks safe construction; they must name the exact downstream build panel they unblock.
If no harness build is required, state the inspected files and evidence proving the existing harness already satisfies the requirement.
The output is incomplete when it identifies a harness gap but assigns no AI agent to build or repair it.
6. SUPPORTING SPRINT MAP
Place this section after all copyable panels.
Include:
MISSION
- what we are trying to accomplish
- why it matters
- what success looks like
TOPICS COVERED
For each topic include status, useful context, and likely repo impact.
Use statuses: decided, partial, unresolved, rejected, or future idea.
DECISIONS MADE
- product decisions
- technical decisions
- naming, contracts, and patterns
- things explicitly rejected
CURRENT STATE
- repo or project
- branch, PR, or sprint
- recent changes
- stale or uncertain context
FACTORING MAP
- harness spine
- skills
- capabilities
- triggers and routing
- conventional application logic
- integration seams
- shared contracts and collision ownership
LINGERING WORK
Classify remaining work into feature, harness, agent-harness factoring, application-logic factoring, cleanup, validation, docs/reporting, runtime proof, research/design, and parallel-safe sprints.
TARGETS TO INSPECT FIRST
Name exact files, directories, scripts, docs, logs, commands, manifests, skills, capabilities, triggers, schemas, workflows, or artifacts.
RISKS AND GAPS
Name unknowns, fragile assumptions, blockers, collision risks, proof gaps, and forbidden scope.
7. QUALITY RULES
Make panels bold, direct, compact, and execution-oriented.
Avoid permission theater, duplicate ownership, trivial-only progress, and giant monolithic prompts.
Do not claim a higher proof level from a lower one.
Do not invent repo facts when evidence is unavailable.
Do not repeat a second launch order after the panels.
